# Clustering {#sec-img-clustering}

<!-- HLC: If things get too long, we might want to split into
1. dimension reduction (transcription-only & spatially aware), 
2. clustering (transcription-only & spatially aware)
3. annotation (including DGE analysis & label transfer)
...or something like that. -->

## Preamble

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache=TRUE)
```

### Introduction

TODO 

<!--Jieran:
* Clustering is an important step for the workflow analysis
* Many methods are focusing on the development of methods based-off scRNA clustering method. But 
imaging-based datasets have a different strategies and cannot make the same assumption as the 
other 
-->

### Dependencies

```{r deps, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(scran)
library(scater)
library(igraph)
library(Banksy)
library(ggspavis)
library(pheatmap)
library(basilisk)
library(patchwork)
library(reticulate)
library(zellkonverter)
library(SpatialExperiment)
# set seed for random number generation
# in order to make results reproducible
set.seed(2025)
# load data from previous section
(spe <- readRDS("img-spe_dr.rds"))
```

## Clustering

<!-- HLC: In a practical I ran, we did spatial dimension reduction (e.g., 'spatialPCA') plus Leiden, and compared that with 'normal' Leiden and spatially aware clustering. Perhaps something like that would be nice conceptually? Ideally, reduced dimensions are already available from an earlier chapter, including transcription-only and spatial PCs. Some of these comparisons could go in collapsed boxes (see also comment below), as to not over-clutter the chapter. -->

### Non-spatial

As a baseline, we will perform graph-based clustering.
Specifically, we construct a shared nearest neighbor (SNN) graph based on non-spatial PCs (`reducedDim` slot `"PCA_tx"`), and use the Leiden algorithm for community detection.

```{r clu-Leiden}
# build cellular shared nearest-neighbor (SNN) graph
g <- buildSNNGraph(spe, use.dimred="PCA_tx", type="jaccard", k=20)
# cluster using Leiden community detection algorithm
k <- cluster_leiden(g, objective_function="modularity", resolution=0.6)
table(spe$Leiden <- factor(k$membership))
```

### Spatially aware

#### Neighborhood-based

<!-- Jieran:
1. Brief introduction of BANKSY principle
2. Introduction of the type of methods that are embedding the spatial information
3. Suggest also other methods with similar line of logic (embedding-based)
-->

`r BiocStyle::Biocpkg("BANKSY")` [@Singhal2024-BANKSY] embeds cells in a product space of their own and their local neighborhood's (average) transcriptome, representing cell state and microenvironment. 
Here, the mixing parameter $\lambda\in[0,1]$ (argument `lambda` in `runBanksyPCA()`), controls the spatial component's weight; e.g., **when $\lambda=0$, `BANKSY` reduces to non-spatial clustering**.

```{r clu-Banksy, message=FALSE}
# retrieve 'lambda' used for dimension reduction
l <- metadata(spe)$BANKSY_params$lambda 
# perform 'Banksy' clustering
spe <- clusterBanksy(spe, dimred="PCA_sp", resolution=0.2)
# reformat cluster assignments
k <- grep("^clust", names(colData(spe)))
names(colData(spe))[k] <- "Banksy"
table(spe$Banksy <- factor(spe$Banksy))
```

<!--HLC: In principle, Python here works but 'SCVI' is way too slow...

#### Encoder-based


1. Introduction of encoder-based methods
2. Introduction of CellCharter methods specifically
3. Other methods: STAGATE PROST SpaceFlow

```{r env, warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# initialize 'basilisk' environment
env <- BasiliskEnvironment(
  envname="CellCharter", pkgname="OSTA",
  channels=c("conda-forge", "pytorch"),
  packages=c(
    "python=3.10.15",
    "pytorch=1.12.1",
    "torchvision=0.13.1",
    "torchaudio=0.12.1"),
  pip=c(
    "scvi-tools==0.20.3",
    "cellcharter==0.2.0",
    "anndata==0.10.9"))
# activate underlying conda environment
use_condaenv(obtainEnvironmentPath(env))
ad <- zellkonverter::SCE2AnnData(spe)
```

```{python CellCharter, eval=FALSE}
import cellcharter as cc
import anndata as ad
import squidpy as sq
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import random
import scvi

obj = r.ad
obj.layers["counts"] = obj.X
seed = 2025
random.seed(seed)
scvi.settings.seed = seed

# variational autoencoder for feature extraction
scvi.model.SCVI.setup_anndata(obj, layer="counts")
mod = scvi.model.SCVI(obj)
mod.train(enable_progress_bar=True, early_stopping=True, early_stopping_patience=10)
obj.obsm["X_scVI"] = modl.get_latent_representation(obj).astype(np.float32)

# getting neighborhood aggregation
sq.gr.spatial_neighbors(obj, library_key="sample", coord_type="generic", 
                        delaunay=True, spatial_key="spatial_fov", percentile=99)
cc.gr.aggregate_neighbors(obj, n_layers=3, use_rep="X_scVI", 
                          out_key="X_cellcharter", sample_key="sample")

# clustering by scanning a range of data number
mod = cc.tl.Cluster(n_clusters=14, random_state=seed)
mod.fit(obj, use_rep="X_cellcharter")

label_df = pd.DataFrame({"label": mod.predict(adata, use_rep="X_cellcharter")}) 
label_df[["label"]].value_counts()
```

```{r load cellcharter results, eval=FALSE}
cc_labels <- read.csv("label_cellcharter.csv")[["label"]]
table(spe$CellCharter_clusters <- as.factor(cc_labels))
```

#### Other methods: Probability statistics-based methods 

* See more example in @sec-seq-clustering-de
* BASS
* DR.SC
-->

## Visualization

<!--Jieran:
* Visualization (Align the cluster labels before running it)
* Smoothness entropy checking
-->

Let's visualize the assignment obtains from non-spatial and spatially aware clustering:

```{r plot-xy-k-joint, message=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=2.5}
#| code-fold: true
# TODO: 'ggspavis' is being a bit pedantic...
# 'in_tissue' missing and duplicated names
spe$in_tissue <- 1
spe$x_centroid <- NULL
spe$y_centroid <- NULL
lapply(c("Leiden", "Banksy"), \(.) {
    plt <- plotSpots(spe, annotate=.)
    plt$layers[[1]]$aes_params$stroke <- 0
    plt$layers[[1]]$aes_params$size <- 0.2
    plt
}) |>
    wrap_plots(nrow=1) &
    theme(legend.key.size=unit(0, "lines")) &
    scale_color_manual(values=unname(pals::trubetskoy())) 
```

Especially in large tissues, the above type of plot makes it difficult to spot rare subpopulations, and might cause cells to overlap in regions with high cellular density.
This can be misleading, as we will tend to see only highly abundant subpopulations, or the cells plotted last and on top (i.e., later columns in the object).

To better distinguish between different subpopulations, we can instead generate separate spatial plots with one subpopulation highlighted at a time:

```{r plot-xy-k-split, fig.width=9, fig.height=6}
#| code-fold: true
lapply(levels(spe$Banksy), \(k) {
    # highlight cells assigned to cluster 'k'
    spe$foo <- spe$Banksy == k
    plt <- plotSpots(spe, annotate="foo")
    plt$layers[[1]]$aes_params$stroke <- 0
    plt$layers[[1]]$aes_params$size <- 0.2
    plt + ggtitle(k)
}) |>
    wrap_plots(nrow=3) &
    scale_color_manual(values=c("lavender", "purple")) &
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5), legend.position="none")
```

<!--
### (Collapse) Effect of configurations on the results

* Cross-methods ARI validation

### (Collapse) Scalability of clusteirng methods on the results

* Using local machine to show the runtime availablility 
* Refer to benchmarking studies for potential scability issue
-->

## Downstream

::: {.callout-note title="PC regression" collapse="true"}

For any single-cell analysis where downstream tasks rely on PCs, it is useful to perform linear regression of (continuous or categorical) covariates of interest onto PCs. 
This quantifies the variance explained by the covariate and can help assess the extend of unwanted variation (due to, e.g., cell area) as opposed to subpopulations driving transcriptional differences. 
Here, we regress total counts, cell area, and cluster assignments against PCs:

```{r pcr}
pcs <- reducedDim(spe, "PCA_tx")
ids <- c("total_counts", "cell_area", "Leiden", "Banksy")
pcr <- lapply(ids, \(id) {
    fit <- summary(lm(pcs ~ spe[[id]]))
    r2 <- sapply(fit, \(.) .$adj.r.squared)
    data.frame(id, pc=seq_along(r2), r2)
}) |> do.call(what=rbind)
```

Here, `Leiden` (transcription-only) and `Banksy` (spatially aware) clusterings perform similar in terms of capturing (spatially unaware) PCs; as to be expected, `Leiden` clusters do slightly better.
Had we set a higher value for $\lambda$ when running `Banksy`, results would diverge more; vice versa, using spatial PCs for regression would have `Leiden` clusters perform worse.
Also, note that we would expect results to converge for $\lambda=0$.

```{r plt-pcr, fig.width=5, fig.height=2.5}
#| code-fold: true
pcr$id <- factor(pcr$id, ids)
pal <- c("magenta", "gold", "cyan", "blue")
ggplot(pcr, aes(pc, r2, col=id)) +
    geom_line(show.legend=FALSE) + geom_point() +
    scale_color_manual("predictor", values=pal) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(1, seq(5, 20, 5))) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0, 1), breaks=seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
    labs(x="principal component", y="coeff. of determination") +
    guides(col=guide_legend(override.aes=list(size=2))) +
    coord_cartesian(xlim=c(1, 20)) +
    theme_minimal() + theme(
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        legend.key.size=unit(0, "lines"))
```

:::

### DGE analysis

For details on identifying genes that are differentially expressed (DE) between groups of cells, we refer readers to [OSCA](https://bioconductor.org/books/3.19/OSCA.basic/marker-detection.html); a standard approach is given below, visualizing the average expression of exemplary markers across clusters:

```{r dge, fig.width=10, fig.height=3}
# differential gene expression analysis
mgs <- findMarkers(spe, groups=spe$Leiden, direction="up")
# select for a few markers per cluster
top <- lapply(mgs, \(df) rownames(df)[df$Top <= 3])
length(top <- unique(unlist(top)))
# average expression by clusters
pbs <- aggregateAcrossCells(spe, 
    ids=spe$Leiden, subset.row=top, 
    use.assay.type="logcounts", statistics="mean")
# visualize averages z-scaled across clusters
pheatmap(mat=t(assay(pbs)), scale="column")
```


## References {.unnumbered}
=======
<!-- I would do this for one of the results only, as to not make it overly complicated for the less proficient readers out there... I think doing a comparison is interesting as well, but might be best to have in a collapsed box, i.e., independent of the main content. The goal being not so much science (e.g., benchmarking), but providing a resource for others.

```{r DEG analysis, eval=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=4}
#| code-fold: true
#| label: fig-charts
#| fig-cap: "Charts"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - "First"
#|   - "Second"
#| layout-ncol: 2

getTopDEGs <- function(spe, clustering) {
  mgs <- findMarkers(spe, groups=spe[[clustering]], direction="up")
  top <- lapply(mgs, \(.) rownames(.)[.$Top <= 1])
  top <- unique(unlist(top))
  top <- top[top %in% rownames(spe)]
  
  pbs <- aggregateAcrossCells(spe, 
    ids=spe[[clustering]], subset.row=top, 
    use.assay.type="logcounts", statistics="mean")
  
  return(pbs)
}
pbsLeiden <- getTopDEGs(spe=spe, clustering="Leiden")
pbsBanksy <- getTopDEGs(spe=spe, clustering="Banksy")

plotHeatmap(pbsLeiden, 
                    colour_columns_by="Leiden", fontsize=9,
                    features=rownames(pbsLeiden), center=TRUE, scale=TRUE,
                    cluster_cols=FALSE, cluster_rows=FALSE)

plotHeatmap(pbsBanksy, 
                    colour_columns_by="Banksy", fontsize=9,
                    features=rownames(pbsBanksy), center=TRUE, scale=TRUE,
                    cluster_cols=FALSE, cluster_rows=FALSE)
```

```{r plot-xy-expr, fig.width=4, fig.height=3, eval=FALSE, message=FALSE}
#| collapse: TRUE
es <- as.matrix(t(logcounts(spe)[rownames(pbsBanksy), ]))
fd <- data.frame(df, es, check.names=FALSE)
gene_plot <- ggplot(fd, aes(x_centroid, y_centroid, col=MZB1)) +
    coord_equal(expand=FALSE) +
    geom_point(shape=16, stroke=0, size=0.2) +
    scale_color_gradientn(colors=c("navy", "red", "gold", "ivory")) +
    theme_xy + theme(legend.key.height=unit(1, "lines"))

cluster_plot <- ggplot(df, aes(x_centroid, y_centroid, col=(Banksy_clusters ==3))) + 
  geom_point(shape=16, stroke=0, size=0.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("navy", "cyan")) + 
  ggtitle("Banksy Cluster 3") + 
  coord_equal(expand=FALSE) +
  theme_void() + 
  theme(legend.position="none",
        plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5),
        panel.background=element_rect(fill="black", color="white"))

gene_plot | cluster_plot
```

### Comparison of DEGs in different spatially-aware clustering cases (collapse)
-->

## Appendix

### Saving {.unnumbered}

```{r save-data}
# default to spatially aware clustering
spe$k <- spe$Banksy; saveRDS(spe, "img-spe_cl.rds")
```

### Benchmarks {.unnumbered}

* [https://doi.org/10.1093/bfgp/elae040]()
* [https://doi.org/10.1186/s13059-024-03361-0]() 
* [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41592-024-02325-3]()
* [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.csbj.2023.11.055]()

### References {.unnumbered}
